topics.title: Time & Space complexity
topics.summary:
- **Time complexity** quantifies the amount of time an algorithm takes to run as a function of the input size, typically represented by N.
- **Space complexity** quantifies the amount of memory an algorithm uses as a function of the input size.
- **Big-O notation (O)** describes the upper bound of an algorithm's growth rate, representing the worst-case scenario.
- **Omega notation (Ω)** describes the lower bound, representing the best-case scenario.
- **Theta notation (Θ)** describes the tight bound, when the best-case and worst-case complexities are asymptotically the same.
- Algorithm analysis often focuses on **worst-case time complexity** to provide a guaranteed maximum performance limit.

mcqs.question: Which asymptotic notation provides an upper bound for the running time of an algorithm?
mcqs.explanation: Big-O notation describes the upper bound of an algorithm's growth rate, indicating the maximum time an algorithm will take.

mcqs.question: An algorithm has a time complexity of O(N log N). If N is doubled, how does the running time approximately change?

options.C: It more than doubles but less than quadruples

mcqs.explanation: If T(N) = N log N, then T(2N) = 2N log(2N) = 2N (log N + log 2) = 2N log N + 2N. This is more than doubling (2N log N) but less than quadrupling.

mcqs.question: Consider an algorithm that iterates through an array of size N once and then performs a binary search on the same array. What is the overall time complexity?
mcqs.explanation: Iterating through the array takes O(N) time. Binary search takes O(log N) time. The dominant term is O(N), so the overall complexity is O(N + log N) which simplifies to O(N).

mcqs.question: Which of the following scenarios would typically require the analysis of the best-case time complexity?

options.A: To guarantee the maximum time an algorithm will ever take.
options.B: To understand the minimum time an algorithm might take under ideal conditions.
options.C: To determine the average performance for typical inputs.
options.D: To compare the scalability of algorithms for large inputs.

mcqs.explanation: Best-case complexity (often denoted by Omega notation) helps understand the minimum time an algorithm needs, typically under ideal input conditions.

topics.title: Complexity Classes and Concepts
topics.summary:
- **P (Polynomial time)**: This class includes decision problems that can be solved by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time.
- **NP (Non-deterministic Polynomial time)**: This class includes decision problems for which a given solution (certificate) can be *verified* in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine.
- **NP-Hard**: A problem H is NP-Hard if every problem L in NP can be reduced to H in polynomial time; meaning H is at least as hard as any problem in NP.
- **NP-Complete (NPC)**: A problem is NP-Complete if it is both in NP and is NP-Hard; these are the 'hardest' problems in NP and are inter-reducible.
- **Cook's Theorem**: A landmark result proving that the Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is NP-Complete, establishing the existence of NP-Complete problems.

mcqs.question: Which statement accurately describes a problem in the P class?

options.A: Its solution can be verified in polynomial time, but not necessarily solved.
options.B: It can be solved by a deterministic algorithm in polynomial time.
options.C: It is at least as hard as any problem in NP.
options.D: It has no known polynomial-time algorithm, but one might exist.

mcqs.explanation: Problems in P are those that can be solved by a deterministic algorithm in polynomial time, implying efficient solvability.

mcqs.question: A problem L is said to be NP-Complete if:

options.A: It can be solved in polynomial time and it is NP-Hard.
options.B: It is in NP and every problem in P can be reduced to it in polynomial time.
options.C: It is in NP and every problem in NP can be reduced to it in polynomial time.
options.D: It is not in NP but is NP-Hard.

mcqs.explanation: An NP-Complete problem must satisfy two conditions: it belongs to NP, and it is NP-Hard (meaning all problems in NP can be polynomially reduced to it).

mcqs.question: Which of the following is a direct implication of Cook's Theorem?

options.B: No NP-Complete problem can be solved in polynomial time.
options.C: The Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is the hardest problem in P.

mcqs.explanation: Cook's Theorem (also known as the Cook-Levin theorem) established that the Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) is NP-Complete, proving the existence of such problems.

mcqs.question: If P = NP, which of the following would be true?

options.A: All problems in NP-Hard would also be in P.
options.C: Every problem whose solution can be efficiently verified can also be efficiently solved.
options.D: Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP) would still be NP-Hard but not in P.

mcqs.explanation: If P = NP, it means that any problem whose solution can be verified in polynomial time (NP) can also be solved in polynomial time (P). This fundamentally changes our understanding of computational limits.
